comment: Nearly a dozen wasps were hovering around the anchor for R1. Set a 40’ rap off a tree a bit farther RDC that dropped me into the narrows just before the second swimmer. Downclimbed the next rap and packed out some old webbing. A couple swimmers, a few deep wades, and some opportunities for fun, moderately challenging downclimbs. Water levels appeared low and lots of algae in some pools.
The technical section was swarming with bees and mosquitoes. Nice, clear water in the pool - swimmer.

This was the first technical canyon for a couple in the group. We did this without wetsuits. The pools were cool, but the warm air temp and sun balanced that out. A bolt has been added RDC on the final rappel which replaced the previous pinch point set up. The 10ish ft. exit climb (off the house sized boulder) has a wasp nest at the top that was disturbed. Avoid them by moving down canyon another 50ft or so and scrambling up RDC to exit.

Great day in the canyon. The log rappel in the begin has a hornet nest in or close to it. Be mindful! Also, a bolt was installed that wasn’t there a month ago when we opened it. Not sure what the point is. It was at the same location as the old redirect for high flow sequence for Lower Davis Falls under the bridge.

Due to recent rainfall, water levels were pretty fun but not overwhelming. All anchors were in good shape, I avoided the bees by using the alt DCL tree. Might have been the last run for 2023, the air temps were quite cool and I def felt the chill towards the end of the canyon even in a dry suit. Amazing place, I can see why everyone goes here, I hope to get out there next season again!! I also did my best to look for trash under the bridge and didn't see anything laying around. I documented this canyon with current conditions and obstacles which I'm sure will change but I will add the vid to the media subpart of this canyon page.

Canyon was as described, and we followed suit with ghosting techniques. There is an abundance of wasps and bees throughout the canyon. One of our group members suffered a half dozen stings. The approach is not as difficult as I thought, the canyon was 5-star, and the hike out is atrocious. Be prepared for a quarter mile of solid bush-whacking before finding a trail. Without having done it, I might recommend following the creek to the private land, then assending to the south ridge to find the trail.

The hike in was not bad and in the fall there were no bees or wasps. Start early the jeep roads to the put in are very hot if it was the middle of the day. The canyon would be crazy in high water. The hike out if you no where your going is 10 minutes of real uphill bushwacking then not bad at all
Good canyon for first timers. We actually had 5 newbies in the group. Some pools but all avoidable. Hundreds of bees in many places

Parked by the exit and followed the kml path to the start (avoiding the road). Scenic canyon, even before the technical section. Pool at the base of R1 was maybe calf-deep. We avoided by going LDC. Between R2 and R3 is a section of walking, and we’d noticed the referenced bees. There’s a dead vulture at the first stage of R3. The second stage can easily be downclimbed when dry. Re-equalized the R4 anchor for a more LDC start as the first person down reported an unpleasant start RDC. Exit was quick and straightforward. Webbing was all in fine condition. Agree that this is a solid canyon for beginners.

The webbing at many of the anchors was crispy and not trustworthy. Flood(s) have buried webbing at a couple places making it impossible to fully inspect and difficult to replace. The next group through might appreciate a trowel as we had to be cautious of a loop of webbing that wanted to lift off a rock horn. Also recommend bringing a lot of webbing (40-50'). Found several anchors where EDKs were tied instead of water knots. One party member was stung by a bee.

First 2 anchors are easily downclimbed or climbed around. The webbing on the mandatory raps looked good, as well as the deadman anchor on rap 4. (At least the 4th rap we did. The rap descriptions are a hot mess, and I can understand why… there’s lots of options and downclimbs and climb arounds, it’s hard to keep track.) Take a 100’ rope and a few feet of webbing and you’ll be grand. No bees this time, lots and lots of mud to negotiate. It was mostly avoidable, but not completely, so we got a little down and dirty. Hitchhiked the car shuttle with great luck. Great, easy, beginner-friendly canyon.

Fun canyon with some really scenic rappels. A landslide has occurred at the base of the last rappel, which may provide a new exit, did not explore too far as there are also ground wasps making their home in the dirt. Definitely bring a change of clothes for the hike out if taking the long route.

Be mindful of Hornets throughout the canyon. Very short narrow section with tall walls were nice. Had some fun downclimbing R2 with partner capture. Exit lower portion thwarckong & mostly screw scramble mid way to top. Notice various fire rings along the keep trail on the exit. Might be more fun doing Canyon out & back with ascend R1. Comparison to nearby short canyonPHog: FW taller narrow section but very short, PH more pools and red slabs

A few shallow, avoidable pools and light trickle over R1. The travertine fall is quite beautiful and unique. Narrows section is short but very pretty. Downclimbed R2 LDC. Exit was fairly painless. Lots of flying critters out and about after the recent rains (wasps, moths, butterflies, grasshoppers, etc.). Underrated canyon!

important: there were about 100 bees nesting in the final "pothole" about 4 feet before the last rap. these were not there the week prior. they were totally unbothered by us, but we had a person in our group who is severely allergic which meant she had to cover up fully, stem across the mess, and execute perfectly. regress would have been possible out of the canyon but we would have been stumbling through choss in the dark and in the rain. made good enough choices. this was not our first choice canyon, but rain in the surrounding area kicked us out of our plans a & b. this canyon only drains the saddle and two peaks of frenchman and has good egress throughout.
Beehive 400 feet after the last slide in the first section of Frye. Went around DLC for 30 feet then got back in the Waterhouse. Keep those eyes peeled and be safe.

Ran Hager and Davis today. Quick updates. Log in R1 pool has been removed. Could be useful to check depth or find a new anchor to go in flow. Removed logs after S2 and pool just before the triple slides. Fun day. WATCH OUT FOR THE YELLOW JACKETS ON THE EXIT. There is a nest and they were active. We were fortunate.

Yellowjackets are out in force this year -- we were attacked on both entrance & exit. Creek itself had low water, but still fun rappelling the falls and floating in all the pools. Reached the bottom of the falls by a good hour, so continued with lower Henline for a full day.

Great day out and anchors are still in good condition. We had a low clearance car and drove most of the way to the trail head. We stopped about .25 before in a pull out near the power lines and the. Hiked in. It seemed the worst part of the road was beyond that (ruts, sand, steep in a section) and had no regrets about adding a little extra walk for the car safety. Watch out for a buried bees nest after R3. It's about 30 ft from the bottom of the rap on the way to the next rap. Easily avoided if you notice, but also could have easily stepped on the hives opening hole.

We had 3 adults and 2 kids. The 3 adults are intermediate level. The kids are brand new in canyoneering. We've practiced in Calico Basin but this is their first actual canyon. They had fun! All anchors were good. Bees were minimal/non-issue.

All anchors in good condition. Safe, fun canyon, with easy approach. There are a considerable number of bees in the canyon and we encountered a swarm at a small chokepoint in the canyon. I only mention in case of allergies, but our group of 7 was unscathed.

I cut out most webbing anchors on obvious jumps. Just meat anchors people down drops who don't want to jump, or use cem/toggle. One 10' drop is technical that we rappelled off the log. There is still an anchor on the last drop which is a technical-ish jump. Theres a bolt, hanger, quicklink, teblyon ring anchor dcr that goes as a slide. (It does look like a slide) Exit is wonderfully great to follow. Will hopefully update the exit track. There are ground wasps on approach and exit. For the approach to stay to the right of the track a bit once you start descending. On the exit, avoid the second steep lose section of the exit trail. I got stung once on the entrance and a teammate for 4 stings on the exit.

After a long approach hike in (that we think was correct given the limited beta), we were surprised to find "not water" canyon full of water! Who would have guessed! Bee wary of yellow jackets, one member of the group was stung on the way in. The bottom of the first rap was full of slippery mud. The pothole mentioned in the beta was fine to escape using a partner assist and rope and careful spotting of the last person doing the ~12 foot hang drop. The second rap had a waist deep pool of water at the bottom. The first two people got wet rappelling, but we were able to do a modified guided rappel off a meet anchor to keep everyone else dry. There were then a series of downclimbs, some ending in pools that were knee or less deep. In the second section, we quickly encountered a difficult pothole escape after the first second-section rappel. A short downclimb/rap led to a chest deep pool with a impossibly slippery climb out. We made an attempt to use our potshots to get past this section, but it required a difficult ~40-50 ft throw that we had trouble with. The crack to throw the potshot looked like a great spot for the potshot to catch, but alas, it wasn't, and one was not enough to stick it. Eventually, we got one potshot over and sent someone back into the pool, but there was not enough stick from the potshot to work. Luckily, we hadn't pulled the rope from the first rap in the second section, so after an hour+ of struggling to get the potshot toss (and two people who had gotten chest deep in water with no wetsuit), we decided to punt the rest of the canyon and make an escape. By the time we got back up to where we had made the entrance hike, it was about 5pm and the sun was setting. This story is a great example of why everyone in the group should know how to ascend if needed! We reversed our entrance hike in the dark, following our GPS track. It was below freezing weather (but luckily there was no wind and we had enough warm layers to be comfortable, even with the group members who had gotten wet). We used two rappels to retrace our entrance hike climbs. The first was to get off of the top of the mesa. This had a tricky overhanging start, and one of the members of our group got a hand caught under the rope and pinched. The second exit hike rappel was to descend the first drainage we had initially climbed up the wall of to get up vertically. At this rappel, we noticed that our rope was frozen. Yikes! This second rap brought us back to the bottom of the side drainage off of the lower gulch trail. We followed this back about 2 miles (only bonking one helmet off a fallen tree hard enough to be knock to the ground) and arrived back at the car near 9pm to find it was ~27F with our shoelaces frozen. Not the aventure we were expecting, but we were safe and not bivvying in the canyon!

Low flow but a great run. Definitely chilly without any neoprene, but manageable! Water temperature was much colder than the Jug which we did the day prior. Webbing was in good shape. Lots of wasps both in canyon and above by the rim exit. Took a fun class IV alternative on the right side of the drainage towards the top of the standard exit route.

5/31/24 - Our rope got stuck on rappel 5, tried for an hour and a half to free it (I did not include this time in the time for us to complete the canyon). We planned to return and run the canyon again with a different rope, but something came up and we were unable to. If you plan on running this canyon and can free my rope I would like to arrange some way for me to come and get it as it is practically brand new. Contact me at (951) 293-4263 if you are interested in retrieving my rope or have retrieved it. I will reward for your efforts. Canyon has very low water right now with some moderately deep pools, most are avoidable with some light scrambling and clever rappelling. The water was cold but not unbearable. The deepest water was after rappel 1 at about chest deep, but it is avoidable. The average water depth in most pools was knee deep. Watch out for aggressive bees on the approach as we parked our car in a pullout on the right about 400ft from the bridge and out of nowhere a small swarm of bees attacked us with a shoot first ask questions later mentality.

Terrific fun in Parkett! Did we open it for the year? Had forgotten how good the big rappel is. Replaced several anchors which, oddly, were missing. They are all in good condition. All jumps are in at current water levels. Watch a large log floating in the hanging pool on the two-tier rappel. Having it come down on a rappeller would be no-bueno. Beware yellowjackets on the exit.

Parkett was stunning and a real treat to run on our way back from the PNW rondy! The flow on the big rappel was nothing short of pure fun, but it was difficult to hear the whistle blasts from up top. Our only grievance was being stung 8 times by yellow jackets on the exit! When you find the rock cairn, you'll see an obvious slope on the left side of the embankment, typically used to climb out. Unfortunately there's a huge wasp nest there, that we didn't see, and my partner got lit up. We found an alternate path up by walking on the fallen trees to the right of it, but you've been warned!
Saw some bees, ran. Ran the canyon. Very chill. Ran into a group in front of us.

A scenic canyon with a noteworthy first rappel. Webbing in good shape. Pulls went smooth. Pools were all avoidable though there was a light trickle over the first rap. A hummingbird paid us a visit for a while, and plenty of other flying creatures (including wasps) were out and about. Exit felt straightforward and was not difficult in terms of the climbing/scrambling. Moved at a leisurely pace.

Time is car to car for First Water, Hog, and Phog. Two hours per canyon. Some shallow, avoidable pools in Phog. About a dozen wasps were congregating around what little water was left at the base of R2. Brushy in the last section of the exit - pants recommended.

Carney Springs Trail is steep and exposed/hot during all times of the year, recommend an early start. Decent routefinding skills required towards the end of the approach. Downclimbed R1, and somehow managed to traverse RDC at the base of R2 and just barely jump the pool. There was a slight trickle running over a few raps. Removed some of the prickly pear at R4. The brush between R4 and R5 is still rough in sections, though I found most of it to be quite forgiving. A few bees at the base of the final rap. They were friendly but slightly agitated when 460’ of rope came crashing down on top of them. Exited LDC on a nice hiking trail.

canyon: Rock Creek (Upper North Fork, South Cascades)
comment: Very low water, just a trickle at most of the falls. Deepest pool was about waist deep, but most were far shallower. Walked through a bee hive on the east side of the creek on the stretch between r3 and r4 and got stung on my legs, arms, and neck. :(

Approach: The hike is short and dry. The approach scramble from the base of the falls is well worn and obvious to follow. Once at the apex you scramble down to the watercourse and, at this point, it becomes very loose and it is easy to knock sizeable rocks down on one another--so take care on this section. Anchors: The is a single bolt on the LDC, but there is also a tree slung with webbing RDC. The webbing is in good shape and was set up as a proper wrap 2, pull 1. The second rap is LDC and consists of two bolts on a chain. It is solid and in good condition. (The first rap can be skipped if you want to down climb to the second anchor) Flow: The water level is very low--disappointingly low if you're looking for a class C adventure--as it is slightly above a trickle. We brought wetsuits (mostly hoping to try out some new ones) but didn't even put them on. We got a little wet by staying in the watercourse but we were kind of trying to get wet at that point. The moss is slick and wet but there is bare rock in places that have good traction. In some of the wet mossy sections wasps are congregating but there moved aside as we came through.

The GPX data on ropewiki is flawless. Get it. Flow level perfect. Higher would be tougher, lower would be less fun. Lots of snakes. Watch your step, wear thick pants or at least thick socks. Endless bushwhacking. You absolutely need long pants, long shirt, gloves, face mask, eye protection. From the confluence up Workman's Creek is a nightmare. Slow, difficult progress. The 5th class climb out is tough and filled with cactus. Impossible with a heavy pack. After free climbing it, I set an anchor at the top and rappelled back down to ascend with packs and my partner. There is no viable camping in the canyon. We bivyed in an uncomfortably tiny spot an inch above the water in Workman's creek, above the tough climb out. Two large scorpions outside our tent. The trail up from Hell's Hole is highly overgrown for the first two miles. Lots of sharp plants and tarantula wasps. Would never attempt this canyon in a day. We camped, with absolutely minimal gear, which I would recommend. This is a hard, difficult and long canyon, with 15+ hours of hard effort of boulder hopping, slippery rocks, bushwhacking and route finding for two hours of the most amazing canyon in Arizona. Worth it.

Great flow, great company, great time. Lots of 4 inch spiders and wasps. Drove a sedan to the trailhead from both directions. Water crossing from the west was passable and maybe a foot deep. Crossing from the east was a wet concrete slab. Water was a great temperature, never needed wool socks, neoprene socks, or wetsuit gloves, though others there reported the water being really cold. Measured 58.2°F. Drank 1L of water.

One swimmer and a few wades. All webbing in good shape. It had rained the prior day so the rock was still wet and choss was more excessive than normal. A fair amount of bee activity around the final couple rappels but they seemed mostly unbothered. Time reflects a casual pace.

If I could, I’d give it 2.5. The nasty brush on the far shore, the chossy climb, and the awkward, yet short first 2 raps are all a pain. But the last rap makes it worth doing at least once, and makes for a quick, if scruffy intro to nearby canyons. I recommend leaving watercraft at home. This is by a friendly place for packrafts or other inflatables. I make sure everyone has a 20L drybag, rolled up with lots of air inside. With that (or some other floaty device) in the pack, backstroking to the other side (and back at the end) is no big deal - and I’m a below average swimmer. Webbing on the short raps is getting crispy, will need replacing soon. Big rap anchor was solid. All pulls easy. Some bees near R4 anchor, but they stayed calm.

Lovely pretty canyon with beautiful waterfalls, some fun sporty flow, a couple of short jumps. Got to see a salamander at the very end. It is not done very often, but it definitely deserves more love. Anchors were in great condition. We got to do some fun problem solving in the lower section, which have some short drops but no fixed anchor material. The approach sucks a bit, beware of hornets. Exit went pretty smoothly

Enjoyable canyon. Not quite as good as Fulton. Starts out a little boring, gets better towards the end. Webbing is in decent condition at all of the anchors. There is a paper wasp nest DCL at the downclimb before the R15 anchor, so treat that with respect.
